notes.anchor: dropped his demand for a triumph
notes.note: A general claiming a triumph had to remain outside the city walls (retaining his imperium) until the senate voted it; by entering Rome Appius forfeited the claim, but removed the pretext of arrogance his prosecutor was exploiting.

notes.anchor: no senatorial decree except about the Latin Festival
notes.note: The Feriae Latinae, the movable festival of the Latin communities on the Alban Mount over which the consuls presided; the joke is that the consuls had passed nothing of substance, only the routine business of fixing the festival.

notes.anchor: Our Curio's tribunate is frozen solid
notes.note: C. Scribonius Curio, tribune of the plebs 50 BC; at this point still aligned against Caesar, his tribunate had so far achieved nothing. He soon switched sides (see section 5).
As curule aedile Caelius policed the markets (tabernarii) and Rome's water supply (aquarii); the quip is that these petty municipal squabbles were the only signs of life in a torpid city.
Curio as tribune had pressed for inserting an intercalary month into the calendar (a pontifical prerogative), partly to prolong his term; thwarted, he went over to Caesar.
P. Servilius Rullus, tribune 63 BC, whose sweeping land bill Cicero had killed in his De Lege Agraria speeches; Caelius means Curio's road-building bill was as demagogic and far-reaching.
Caelius, preparing the games for his aedileship, repeatedly badgers Cicero (governor of a province rich in big cats) to procure panthers for the beast-shows in the arena.
